coredata.dc:title: Plausible low cost MII/MIII-LDH@AMCD as adsorbent for iron reduction from polluted river water

coredata.dc:description: Sorption study of heavy metals, specifically iron, from aqueous solutions and river water was conducted using Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs) and functionalized with Aegle Marmelos carbon dots. Four different types of adsorbent were synthesized: ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH, and CaAl LDH@AMCD. The incorporation of carbon dots enhances the sorption efficiency of these LDHs materials. Adsorption experiments for the removal of iron ions was evaluated by the phenanthrolic method, a colorimetric technique known for its sensitivity in detecting trace iron concentrations. Successful incorporation of green carbon dots was confirmed through TEM, PL and TRPL analysis techniques. Results revealed that the addition of carbon dots significantly improved the iron adsorption capacity of both ZnAl and CaAl LDHs, with ZnAl LDH@AMCD showing the highest efficiency. Comparative analysis demonstrated that ZnAl LDH@AMCD showed better sorption performance than their non-modified counterparts due to increased surface area and enhanced interaction sites for metal ions. This study highlights the potential of LDH@CD composites as efficient, low-cost adsorbents for removing iron from water, offering a promising approach for water treatment applications.

We would like to express our gratitude to Professor Hilloljyoti Singha, Department of Zoology, Bodoland University for his valuable advice.

head.ce:title: Plausible low cost MII/MIII-LDH@AMCD as adsorbent for iron reduction from polluted river water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Bodoland University, Kokrajhar, Assam, India, 783370
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Bodoland University, Kokrajhar, Assam, India, 783370

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the adsorption of iron ions by prepared adsorbent.

Sorption study of heavy metals, specifically iron, from aqueous solutions and river water was conducted using Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs) and functionalized with Aegle Marmelos carbon dots. Four different types of adsorbent were synthesized: ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH, and CaAl LDH@AMCD. The incorporation of carbon dots enhances the sorption efficiency of these LDHs materials. Adsorption experiments for the removal of iron ions was evaluated by the phenanthrolic method, a colorimetric technique known for its sensitivity in detecting trace iron concentrations. Successful incorporation of green carbon dots was confirmed through TEM, PL and TRPL analysis techniques. Results revealed that the addition of carbon dots significantly improved the iron adsorption capacity of both ZnAl and CaAl LDHs, with ZnAl LDH@AMCD showing the highest efficiency. Comparative analysis demonstrated that ZnAl LDH@AMCD showed better sorption performance than their non-modified counterparts due to increased surface area and enhanced interaction sites for metal ions. This study highlights the potential of LDH@CD composites as efficient, low-cost adsorbents for removing iron from water, offering a promising approach for water treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the growing consciousness of environmental sustainability, the concern for heavy metal pollution has accumulated significant attention across society. The proximity of excessive heavy metals put forward serious risks to both environment and human health [1–3]. The pertinacious nature of heavy metals inhibits their biodegradation leading to their agglomeration within the biological systems though the food chains, which can have harmful effects on human health [4]. Rapid increment in the advancements of industrial and technological sectors has significantly contributed to the elevated release of heavy metals into the environment primarily due to human activities [5–7]. Over the past few decades, vivid efforts have been devoted for the betterment of eco-friendly and cost-efficient methods, often termed “green technologies” casted at alleviating heavy metal pollution in soil and water, especially from industrial water discharge [8,9]. Water contamination by heavy metals is a suggestive global concern which threatens both environmental and human health. Toxic metals including Iron (Fe), Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Mercury (Hg), Arsenic (As), Chromium (Cr), Copper (Cu) and Zinc (Zn) are harmful particularly due to their endurance in the environment, conferring to long-term ecological degradation [10–12]. These pollutants primarily roll out from various industrial processes including mining, electroplating, battery production and the release of untreated industrial waste [13]. Once introduced into ecosystem, heavy metals interpolate water systems thereby accumulating in sediments and thus they are absorbed by aquatic life eventually entering the human food chain [17]. This bioaccumulation leads into severe chronic health issues, including neurological impairments, cardiovascular complications, kidney disordes and cancers. Such calculated high risks highlights the critical need for efficient and sustainable remediation approaches [14–18].Scheme 1. .

Iron an element occurs naturally in rocks and soils which under precise conditions can leach into water resources. However, it often routes to water discoloration, affecting plumbing fixtures, dishware and clothing giving water yellowish to reddish hue when it surpasses recommended thresholds. Consequently, it mutates the taste and odour of drinking water not only creating aesthetic challenges but also poses indirect health risks [19–22]. Iron toxicity can lead to several health problems, including anorexia, oliguria, diarrhea, hypothermia, diphasic shock, metabolic acidosis and in severe cases death [23]. To address these concerns, the U.S Environmental Protection Agency has set a secondary drinking water standard of 0.3 mg/L for iron. Therefore, it is essential to remove this heavy metal from water supplies using effective treatment technologies before being released into the environment. As a result multiple techniques have been developed for the disposal of heavy metals from aqueous solutions utilizing the physical, chemical and biological approaches [24,25]. Commonly illustrious methods for extracting metal ions from water includes chemical precipitation, filtration, ion exchange, electrochemical treatments, membrane technologies, adsorption onto activated carbon and evaporation among others. Regardless of their effectiveness, application of these traditional methods is often limited by technical challenges or economic limitations. Among these adsorption has gained significant consideration as a highly efficient method for removal of heavy metals from the aqueous solutions. This process implicates the entrapping of contaminants on the surface of adsorbent material, facilitating their extraction from the liquid phase [26–28]. The method is treasured for its simplicity, high removal efficiency, cost-effectiveness and capacity to treat large volumes of water with modest energy requirements. The success of adsorption relies heavily on the choice of appropriate adsorbent materials that possess high surface area, favourable surface properties and a strong affinity for heavy metal ions [29,30].

Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs) are a class of 2D layered anionic clays. They are also known as Hydrotalcite-like compounds and are characterized by brucite-like layers, having interlayer anions for charge balance and solvent molecules. The structure of LDH consists of positively charged metal hydroxide layers with charge-balancing anions intercalated between them [31–33]. The general formula for LDHs can be represented as
[M2+ 1-xM3+ x(OH)2 An− x/n·yH2O]

where M2+ and M3+ are divalent and trivalent metal cations, respectively, and An
− is the interlayer anion. They have been considerably applicative for the removal of various contaminants, including heavy metals. The subjective anion-intercalated structure offers several advantages: (a) the interlayer anion type can be adjusted through anion exchange; (b) metal cation present within the layers are tunable, therefore allowing heavy metals to be immobilized within the LDH lattice via a highly stable mineralization process; (c) abundance of –OH groups on the LDH layers furnishes myriad of binding sites; and (d) numerous functional groups such as –SH, –COOH and NH2 can be introduced on the surface or within the interlayer, thus promoting the capture of heavy metals through coordination. These structural traits with the help of different mechanisms such as surface adsorption, stable mineralization, coordination with interlayer anions and anion exchange allows LDH to adsorb heavy metals [34–40].

In recent times, the appeal of green chemistry has grown significantly due to its emphasis on cleanliness and sustainability. Within this framework, the preparation of carbon dots utilizing non-toxic solvents and cost-effective green materials emerges as a pivotal consideration in devising a green synthetic approach [41,42]. Notably, studies have showcased the utilization of natural sources such as carica juice and pomelo peel, yielding carbon dots with quantum yields of 7.0 % and 6.9 % respectively. Similarly, the employment of citrus limonene juice in NaOH solution has yielded carbon dots with a quantum yield of 12.1 % [43–45]. Furthermore, nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from silkworm chrysalis have been successfully synthesized, showcasing their applicability in cell imaging [46]. Pure honey has also been demonstrated as a viable carbon source for carbon dot synthesis in the presence of H2O2 [47,48]. Throughout these preparations, the integration of green materials with chemical reagents, such as acids, bases, or reductants, has been common practice. The choice of appropriate carbon precursors stands out as crucial in modulating or enhancing the properties of carbon dots.

Consequently, there exists a keen interest in fabricating tailored functional composite-based LDHs to augment the adsorption sites available for hydrogen bonding, thereby enhancing their overall adsorption capacity. Employing a strategy of fabricating composites through the assembly of positively charged layered double hydroxides (LDHs) and green carbon dots possessing abundant oxygen-containing functional groups holds significant promise for contaminant removal. Although LDH surfaces inherently exhibit numerous hydroxyl groups, only the unoccupied hydroxyl groups within the LDH sheets are capable of forming hydrogen bonds [49–52]. In this research, we have synthesized ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH and modified them using carbon dots ascertained from Aegle Marmelos leaves (ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH@AMCD). We then explored the adsorption parameters for the removal of iron (Fe) from both aqueous solutions and river water. The Brahmaputra river in Assam, India was opted as the water source and its initial parameters were acquired from the Public Health Engineering Department (PHED). The results from the analysis revealed that the iron concentration in the river water exceeded permissible limits.

Accordingly, our synthesized adsorbent was employed to examine the removal of iron directly from the river as well as from laboratory-prepared solutions. It was further justified from the findings that the adsorbent exhibited significant iron removal efficiency, even in the presence of various competing ionic species, in the natural river water. The adsorbent was thoroughly characterized using techniques such as PXRD, SEM & EDX, FTIR, HRTEM, BET, TGA, TRPL, PL. Additionally the adsorption parameters were systematically studied. It was observed that the incorporation of carbon dots into the LDH matrix introduces additional hydroxyl groups onto the LDH surface, thereby facilitating the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carbon dots. Consequently, the adsorption efficiency of the resulting LDH-carbon dot composite assembly is substantially enhanced, owing to the cooperative effects of hydrogen bonding between carbon dots, alongside electrostatic attraction between LDH. Furthermore the prepared adsorbent with green carbon dots are highly preferable as because LDH known for their high surface area can selectively adsorb iron ions even at low concentrations as compared to other conventional methods. Also the adsorbents prepared are environmentally benign with minimal usage of harmful chemicals making them a greener alternative and demonstrates a good regeneration potential allowing for multiple cycles use. The adsorbents when combined with green carbon dot solution, reduces the long-term cost and waste production and also it exhibits additional functionalities like photoluminescence.

The Aegle marmelos leaves were collected from the local place Kokrajhar, Assam, India. Aluminium Chloride(AlCl3), Zinc chloride(ZnCl2), NaOH, NaHCO3, Calcium Chloride (CaCl2) and was procured from Merck. The obtained chemicals are of analytical grade and used without any further purification. The required solution in the experiment was prepared by deionised water. The river water was collected from Brahmaputra River, Dhubri, Assam.

Zn-Al LDH was prepared via co-precipitation method. 2 M of Zinc Chloride (ZnCl2) solution and 1 M of Aluminium Chloride (AlCl3) solution was taken and mixed in a beaker. Simultaneously, 1 M of NaHCO3 was added in the resultant metal precursor solution in which 2 M of NaOH solution was added in order to maintain pH of 11. The resultant solution was left undisturbed and left for decantation for 24 h. It was then centrifuged at 1500 rpm and the re-dispersed in distilled water and then centrifuged again. This was done in order to remove any impurities left. And it was then dried in hot air oven at 70⁰C and then collected. The resultant white powdered substance is the ZnAl LDH. For the preparation of CaAl LDH 4 M Calcium chloride and 2 M Aluminium Chloride (AlCl3) solution was taken and the above procedure was followed maintaining the pH of 12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots using Aegle marmelos
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon dots using Aegle marmelos(AM) leaves were conducted by the pyrolysis method. Aegle marmelos commonly known as Bael were meticulously cleansed with distilled water to eliminate any potential impurities. Subsequently, the leaves underwent a thorough shade-drying process until complete dehydration was achieved. Following this, they were carbonized at a temperature of 200⁰C for 2 h. After cooling down at room temperature they were grounded into fine powder using mortar and pestle. Then 3 gm of the obtained sample was then dispersed in 25 mL of distilled water and was ultrasonicated to achieve a homogeneous solution. The resulting extract was subjected to filtration and centrifugation procedure which was repeated 2–3 times to eliminate any fine suspended particles. The filtrate was further purified with a filtration membrane and stored at a lower temperature for subsequent applications.

The LDH-carbon dot solution was synthesized employing the colloidal deposition method. Initially, 3gm of Zn-Al LDH and CaAl LDH were meticulously combined with 6 mL of the carbon dot solution and subjected to vigorous stirring at ambient temperature utilizing a magnetic stirrer. Subsequent to the stirring process, the mixture was then permitted to settle undisturbed for approximately 60 min, facilitating thorough interaction. Following this incubation period, the resultant mixture was centrifuged and washed thoroughly with deionized water for three to four times and then dried in an oven set at 60⁰C, culminating in the removal of residual moisture, and was subsequently stored for subsequent applications. The resultant modified adsorbents were named as ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD.

Several characterization techniques were used to characterize the synthesized adsorbents. The PXRD diffractogram was recorded from Rigaku Ultima IV X-Ray powder diffractometer at an accelerating voltage of 35 kV using Cuα λ = 1.54 Å as X-Ray source. The PXRD spectrum was recorded in the 2θ range from 0 to 80. The external morphology was observed by SEM using Zeiss and the percentage composition of the constituent elements by EDX. The specific surface area and pore volume was measured by conducting N2-adsorption–desorption with BET surface area and BJH method by using Quantachrome, Autosorb-IQ MP. The FTIR spectra was carried out using IR-Alpha Bruker by taking KBr as a reference background under the frequency range of 0–4000 cm−1. The thermal stability of the adsorbent was investigated with TGA (Mettlab Toledo). For the measurement of the heavy metal concentration UV-Spectrophotometer-3375 (Electronics India) was utilized. The internal morphology was discerned by using TEM (JOEL JEM-2100) under the accelerating potential of 200 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) was analysed using Photoluminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse Agilent). Time-Resolve Photoluminescence was analyzed using Pico-seconf Lifetime Spectrophotometer (Model: Lifespec II, Edinburgh Instruments). During the experiment a series of 20 mL NaCl (0.1 M) solution was poured in a conical flask, and the pH value of the solution was varied from 2 to 12. Eventually, 0.15 g of the adsorbents were dispersed in the solution and shaken at room temperature for a time period. Afterwards, the suspension was filtered to separate the adsorbent and the final pH of the supernatant liquid was measured by digital pH meter (EI). The PZC value can be estimated from the point of intersection of line which is obtained from the graph of pH initial vs pH final.

The adsorption efficacy of the proposed adsorbents was investigated utilizing a batch equilibrium method at a controlled room temperature. In order to conduct isotherm studies, a range of heavy metal solutions with initial concentrations ranging from 20 mg/L to 160 mg/L were prepared in conical flask bottles, each containing 50 mL of solution. Subsequently 0.025 g of the respective adsorbents were dispersed within the heavy metal solution followed by agitation in a thermostatic shaker. The batches underwent agitation for duration of 3 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Upon completion of the agitation period, the resultant heavy metal solution were subjected to centrifugation to separate the adsorbent material, and the filtrate was subjected to analysis utilizing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to ascertain the concentration of the heavy metal. The removal of iron ions from the aqueous solution was conducted following the 1,10-Phenanthrolic method and the quantity of Iron (Fe) adsorbed onto the adsorbents was determined utilizing an appropriate mathematical equation 1:

For the kinetics experiment, 0.025gm of the adsorbents was combined with 40 mL of heavy metal solution with initial concentration 60 mg/L. The resulting metal solution underwent agitation in a thermostatic shaker, followed by the withdrawl of 2 mL of liquid suspension at predetermined time interval. Subsequently, the quantity of the heavy metal adsorbed onto the adsorbent was analyzed over a reaction time ranging from 0 to 180 min. Upon completion of each time interval, the adsorbent was separated from the metal solution via filtration and its absorbance value was measured with the help of UV–visible spectrophotometer at the wavelength corresponding to the maximum absorption (λ-max) of the heavy metal (Iron). Adsorption isotherm plays a vital role in enhancing our understanding of the interactions between adsorbates and adsorbents. It provides valuable insights into how solute molecules are distributed at the interface between solid and liquid phases, which is essential for designing effective adsorption processes. To analyze experimental data and identify the most suitable model for specific systems, researchers utilize various adsorption isotherm models. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models are among the most widely used. The Langmuir model specifically proposes that monolayer adsorption occurs on a uniform surface with identical adsorption sites [56]. This foundational concept allows for a clearer understanding of adsorption dynamics, and the Langmuir equation can be articulated as follows:
(4)

where Ce denotes the equilibrium concentration, qm denotes the maximum monolayer adsorption capacity, K1 signifies Langmuir constant and qe stands for the quantity of heavy metal adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/L).

The Freundlich isotherm model is based on the principle that the surface of the adsorbent contains heterogeneous sites, which reflect the potential distribution of adsorption sites and energy. This model encompasses a complex set of adsorption processes, with the adsorption mechanism significantly influenced by the constants n and Kf [56]. The heterogeneity of the solid surface is associated with 1/n in this context. The equation is expressed as follows:

The Temkin isotherm model, adsorption heat of incoming adsorbate molecules linearly decreases with increase in coverage of adsorption sites on surface of adsorbent. This model characterizes the adsorption system by uniform distribution of binding energy upto certain maximum value [32]. The equation form of Temkin isotherm model can be given by:

Adsorbate molecules, which possess transferable characteristics and a time-dependent rate of adsorption within the adsorption system, can be effectively analyzed through kinetic studies. Therefore, it is essential to examine the kinetic parameters and the mechanisms involved in evaluating the performance of adsorbents. The experimental studies and their findings were analyzed and interpreted using the four kinetic models described below to assess their rate-limiting processes.

The Pseudo First Order (PFO) model provides valuable insights into the adsorption process by indicating that the difference between the amount of solute adsorbed and its equilibrium capacity is directly proportional to the rate of adsorption. This model effectively highlights the interplay between overall adsorption dynamics and the molecular interactions that govern adsorption kinetics [56]. The non-linear form of the equation can be expressed as follows:
(7)

Where qt and qe represents the quantity of dye adsorbed at equilibrium and specific time (t) respectively.

Building on the principles of chemisorption, the Pseudo Second Order model emphasizes the exchange or sharing of electrons between the adsorbate and adsorbent molecules. This model reveals that the initial phase of the adsorption process is primarily driven by chemical interactions, which facilitate the transfer of electrons between the adsorbent and the contaminant [56]. The equation can be articulated as:
(8)

where qe and qt are the amount of metal ions adsorbed (mmol/g) at equilibrium and at time t, respectively; k2 is the pseudo-second-order rate constant of adsorption(g mmol−1 min−1).

In cases where internal diffusion of particles acts as the rate-limiting factor in the adsorption process, the intraparticle diffusion model can be utilized for assessment. One prominent approach within this framework is the Web-Morris model, which underscores how the movement of adsorbate molecules through the pores of the adsorbent can significantly influence the rate-determining step of adsorption [56]. The equation governing the intraparticle diffusion model can be expressed as:
(9)

Where Ki and I represent the intraparticle diffusion constant and boundary layer thickness respectively.

To gain a deeper understanding of the chemisorption characteristics within the adsorption system, the Elovich model was applied to analyze the experimental kinetic data. This model is particularly useful for examining chemisorption phenomena, as it aims to estimate the activation and deactivation energy of the system. The Elovich kinetic model effectively investigates the interaction between the contaminant and the heterogeneous surface of the adsorbent [56]. By adhering to second-order kinetics, this model considers the non-uniform energy distribution across the solid surface and can be represented by the following equation:
(10)

Where qt stands for the amount of heavy metals adsorbed onto the adsorbent at time t, β represents the limit of surface coverage (g/mg), α denotes the initial sorption rate (mg/g.min).

All adsorption experiments, including adsorption capacity (qe) and removal efficiency (%), were performed in triplicates to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the data. The mean values of the adsorption parameters were calculated, and the errors were quantified using standard deviation (SD). The standard deviation for each parameter was determined using the formula:
