RMSE was used as a supplementary error function alongside R2 to assess the goodness of fit, as it quantitatively represents the average deviation between experimental and model-predicted values.

The PXRD pattern of ZnAl LDH@AMCD, ZnAl LDH, CaAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH is illustrated in Fig. 1 . ZnAl LDH [Fig. 1 (b)], indicative of the basal reflections at (003) and (006) reveals the characteristics of layered structure. These peaks confirm the interlayer spacing, which is crucial for the material's anion exchange capacity and its ability to host intercalated species. The presence of higher-order peaks such as (104), (009), (113), (024), (208), and (119) signifies a well-ordered and crystalline structure, reflecting the arrangement of metal hydroxide layers and the ordered stacking within the material. These peaks indicate that ZnAl LDH exhibits good crystallinity and a stable layered framework. In comparison, the PXRD spectrum of ZnAl LDH modified with green leaves carbon dots (ZnAl LDH@AMCD) Fig. 1 (a) retains the basic layered structure, as seen by the persistence of the (003) and (006) reflections. However, the appearance of new peaks, such as (012), (015), (018), and (110), suggests structural changes due to the modification. These new peaks imply alterations in the interlayer distances and rearrangement of the layers, potentially indicating the incorporation of carbon dots into the structure. The absence of higher-order reflections like (208) in ZnAl LDH@AMCD suggests a reduction in crystallinity, likely due to the disruption caused by the carbon dots. This change in crystallinity could influence the material’s properties, affecting its surface area, adsorption capacity, and ion-exchange behavior.

In case of CaAl LDH Fig. 1 (d), the peaks displayed characteristics peaks of pristine LDH having the indexed plane corresponding to each peaks at 003, 006, 009, 113, 024, 018 and 214 respectively. The peaks at 003, 006 and 009 are associated with the basal spacing of the LDH structure and the second order reflection providing insights for the presence of interlayer anions, cations and thickness of the layers. This obtained result is consistent with that of typical LDH structure. The peaks at 113 and 024 corresponds to the lattice parameter along the plane and the structural regularity of the pristine LDH thereby confirming the material’s crystallinity, while the peak at 018 highlights the structural integrity of the arrangement of the layers. Finally the peak at 214 marks the complexity of the layered arrangement and further supporting the effective placement of the ions and validating the well ordered structure of the material. However upon modification with carbon dot solution CaAl LDH@AMCD Fig. 1 (c) having the corresponding planes at 003, 006, 200, 009, 113, 220, 018 and 214 respectively. From the figure it is clearly seen that even upon mixing with green carbon dot solution the peaks at 003, 006 and 009 represents the typical characteristics of the LDH structure alongwith its basal spacing and layered thickness. This suggests that even after the modification the structure of CaAl LDH@AMCD is not ruptured. The peak occurring at 200, 220 is indicative of the structural changes due to carbon dots indicating possible changes in the surface structure. Nevertheless the peaks at 113, 018 and 214 are indicative of the result that the lattice parameters, structural integrity and placement of the ions within the hydroxide layers remains unaffected even in the presence of the carbon dots.

These findings authenticate that the inclusion of green leaf carbon dots to the pristine ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH does not accord to the original layered double hydroxide structure, while potentially conveying additional functional properties from the carbon dots. The PXRD results give us a comprehensive understanding of the crystalline characteristics and thereby confirms the successful combination of pristine LDHs with green leaf carbon dots.

The external surface morphology of the ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH were examined with the help of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis as depicted in Fig. 2 . The unmodified ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH [ Fig. 2 (A & D)] revealed a porous structure with interconnected pores circulated around the material’s surface. The SEM micrographs of both the crude ZnAl LDH, CaAl LDH imparts the existence of specific plate like structures, which are the characteristics of layered structure of LDHs. For the removal of iron ions from the aqueous solutions these pores play a vital role in the material’s functionality. The porous nature of the adsorbent material implements high surface area which is pivotal for maximizing the adsorption capacity. Iron ions upon interaction with the ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH surface, the pores operate as active sites facilitating their capture and removal from the solution. This allows for the adequate diffusion of the iron ions into the internal structure pointing to the enhanced adsorption kinetics and overall adsorption efficiency. Upon modification of the ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH with Aegle Marmelos carbon dot solution noticeable change in the morphology was observed with the pores in the ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD [ Fig. 2 (B & C)] structure. Although the slight reduction in porosity might suggest in reduction potential, the modification has also introduced new structural features such as flower like pattern shapes on the surface of the structure. This occurs as a result of the interaction between the ZnAl LDH, CaAl LDH and the carbon dots during the modification process.

Upon introduction of the carbon dots into the ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH they interact with the hydroxide layers with the help of various forces such as Van der Waals force, hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. These enhances the surface roughness with the introduction of additional active sites thereby facilitating the interaction between the iron ions and ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH@AMCD leading to improved adsorption performance. Thus it can be concluded that modification of ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD with carbon dots altered its surface morphology with the significant changes in the structural features leading to potential shift in adsorption mechanism. These plate like structures which are aligned in a stacked manner, are thin with well-defined edges reflecting the natural two-dimensional structure of layered Double Hydroxide materials. Interestingly, in many regions of the SEM images flower like patterns are observed which are the characteristics as a result of the interactions between green carbon dots and both the pristine LDHs. During the synthesis process, LDH layers commences in a more organized and symmetrical manner where the carbon dots typically serves as nucleation centres. This synergy causes the LDH layers to bend and curve forming petal-like structures. The green carbon dots representative of their high surface area alongwith their small size might convince local curvature including structural rearrangement in the LDH layers, leading to the formation of these aesthetically flower like morphologies. Despite the formation of flower like structures, the elemental plate like morphology remains intact indicating that the structural morphology of the LDH is not ruptured.

The EDX analysis of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD and their elemental composition is represented in Table 1 . The initial EDX analysis of ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH reveals compelling presence of Chlorine (Cl) in the material. This aspects to the use of chloride salts at the time of synthesis procedure or as a residue from intercalating chloride anions within the LDH layers. Upon intermixing of both the pristine LDHs with the carbon dot solution EDX analysis indicated a notable decrease in the chlorine content. Also concurrently, new peaks corresponding to Carbon (C) appeared in the spectrum suggesting the successful modification of ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH with the carbon dot solution. Carbon dots having an advantage of surface functional groups like carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) may expedite an ion exchange process where chloride ions (Cl−) are replaced by other anions such as hydroxide ions (OH−). Also they can chemically interact with the LDH layers thereby modifying the surface and replacing the chloride anions with oxygen containing groups. The increase in oxygen content in ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD after modification is more likely due to the oxygen enriched surface groups present in the Carbon dots. This increment is particularly due to the new bond formation between Carbon dots and LDH layers which is associated with the inclusion of additional oxygen atoms within the material. This increase in the presence of oxygen in ZnAl LDH@AMCD is indicative of successful functionalization and a potential enhancement in materials reactivity. Overall the EDX analysis confirmed the presence of carbon dots in both the pristine LDHs and the details of the other elemental composition are well elucidated in Table 1.

The shape, size and internal morphology of the as prepared adsorbent was analyzed with the help of Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) which is depicted in Fig. 3 and is of different resolution. The HRTEM images of the ZnAl LDH@AMCD Fig. 3 (b) and CaAl LDH@AMCD Fig. 3 (d) disclose the presence of well defined carbon dots in the range of 10 nm and 8 nm respectively. The well dispersed carbon dots contributed to the overall increase in the surface area which is crucial for enhanced adsorption capacity. However in case of pristine ZnAl LDH Fig. 3 (a) and CaAl LDH Fig. 3 (c) noticeable were platelets portrayed by their layered structure. These type of platelets displays clear lattice fringes signifying the crystalline nature of the material. This revealed a well ordered stacked morphology of the LDH structure thereby confirming its pristine structure without modification. One of the most compelling remark observed from the HRTEM analysis is the embodiment of small distinct, dotted structures on the surface of ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD platelets. These dotted spots are evenly distributed across the surface. The sizes typically varies from few nanometers to tens of nanometers. The recognition of these high contrast dots from the HRTEM images indicates that the carbon dots are successfully incorporated on the surface of the surrounding LDH matrix. The existence of carbon dots amplifies the functionality of the pristine LDHs probably improving the sorption properties. Further, the interaction between the CDs and LDH surface can lead to charge transfer and other synergistic effects which are benign for various applications.

The surface functionalities of the two adsorbents ZnAl LDH@AMCD and ZnAl LDH is represented in Fig. 4
. The corresponding functional groups of the pristine ZnAl LDH [ Fig. 4 (b)] represents a broad peak at 3479 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (O–H) illustrating the presence of water molecules in the adsorbent. The bending vibrations of the carbonate ions (CO3 2−) commonly found in the interlayer spaces of Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs) is exhibited by the peak at wavenumber 1509 cm−1. The sharp peaks at 1360 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of the interlayer NO3 − groups. Also the peaks corresponding to 948 cm−1, 833 cm−1 and 612 cm−1 corresponds to the various M−O stretching (metal–oxygen) and bending modes and thus reflecting the structure of LDH structure. However upon mixing the pristine LDH with carbon dots, a broad adsorption band as a result of the hydrogen bonding interaction between the carbon dots and ZnAl LDH around 3600–3100 cm−1 which is centered at 3402 cm−1. This peak was ascribed to the amine and hydroxyl groups of ZnAl LDH@AMCD [ Fig. 4 (a)]. The peaks corresponding to the wavenumbers 1635 cm−1, 1509 cm−1, 1368 cm−1 and 1051 cm−1 represents the C–N, C═C of aromatic, C–N and bending vibratios of C–O–C of carbon dots which further confirmed the formation of ZnAl LDH@AMCD indicating that the carbon dots have been successfully loaded into ZnAl LDH. While the peaks at 948 cm−1, 833 cm−1 and 612 cm−1 are ascribed to the various M−O stretching (metal–oxygen) and also the bending modes of pristine LDH structure and thereby confirming that the structure of the LDH has not been ruptured due upon modification with carbon dots.

The FTIR analysis of CaAl LDH [ Fig. 4 (d)] represents a broad band at 3442 cm−1 is ascribed to O − H stretching vibrations representing hydroxyl groups in the LDH layers and water molecules alongwith the contributions of hydroxyl group from carbon dots. The peaks at 1639 cm−1 attributes to the bending vibrations of water molecules at interlayer spaces, while the adjacent peak at 1568 cm−1 is a characteristics of C═C stretching, validating the retention of carbon dots aromatic structure. The band at 1366 cm−1 is affiliated with the symmetric stretching vibration of NO3 −. The bands occurring at 773 cm−1 and 594 cm−1 corresponds to metal–oxygen (M−O) stretching vibrations of Ca-O and Al-O bonds in the CaAl LDH. However upon mixing the CaAl LDH [ Fig. 4 (c)] with green carbon dots the broad sharp peak shifting to 3449 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (O–H) which indicates that the water molecules are present in the prepared the presence of water molecules in the adsorbent. The peaks corresponding to the wavenumbers 1639 cm−1, 1380 cm−1 corresponds to C–N, C═C of aromatic, C–N and the peak at 1063 cm−1 illustrates bending vibrations of C–O–C of CaAl LDH@AMCD indicating that the carbon dots have been successfully loaded into CaAl LDH. While the peaks at 963 cm−1, 797 cm−1, 685 cm−1 and 553 cm−1 are ascribed to the various M−O stretching (metal–oxygen) and also the bending modes of pristine LDH structure and thereby confirming that the structure of the LDH has not been ruptured due upon modification with carbon dots.

The surface characteristics like surface area, pore size, pore volume of the following adsorbents ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH, CaAl LDH@AMCD were analyzed were investigated using the BET method. The properties particularly the porosity, surface area they are crucial parameters which are important and provide invaluable insights for the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent material. Comprehending these features helps provide better definition for the materials potential for various applications. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm curve of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD is depicted in Fig. 5
(a), (b), (c), (d). The BET(Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) surface area analysis of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD disclose vivid differences in surface properties before and after modification with carbon dots. Both the pristine ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH exhibits a surface area of 57.98 m2/g and 53.54 m2/g Fig. 5 (a), (c). This value implies a relatively moderate surface area which is representative of the Layered Double Hydroxides (LDH) used in various applications.

Upon mixing of the pristine LDH with carbon dot solutions, the surface area of both ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD increases significantly to 173.525 m2/g and 120.428 m2/g Fig. 5 (b), (d) the sharp enhancement in the surface area can be ascribed to the successful deposition of the carbon dot onto both the LDH layers, resulting in a more porous structure. This occurs as a result of the carbon dots which reforms the materials accessibility to reactants and its overall adsorption capacity, making it more effective for removal of iron ions. Fig. 5 (e), (f), (g), (h) represents the pore size distribution of the adsorbents based on BJH analysis method. From the pore size distribution curve it has been found that the pristine ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH having their pore size measured at 6.85 nm and 3.38 nm having a relatively low pore volume of 0.071 cc/g and 0.047 cc/g. This signifies that the material with more compact structure have limited internal void space thereby leading to smaller pores. This smaller pore volume implies that both pristine LDH has fewer active sites/voids for adsorption, which limits its adsorption efficiency. In contrast upon mixing with green carbon dots, both ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD exhibits larger pore size of 7.75 nm and 4.7 nm with an adequate increased pore volume of 0.479 cc/g and 0.358 cc/g ( Table 2
). This increment in pore size and volume is likely due to the carbon dot within the LDH structure. This further causes an expansion of the layered structure, routing to a more open network with larger voids. This also suggests that the carbon dots enhances the overall porosity of the adsorbent improving its ability to accommodate more no of iron ions.

Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) was conducted on ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD in order to estimate the material’s thermal stability and decomposition. This analysis aims to comprehend the effects of carbon dot intercalation on the LDH structure with a particular focus on the minimal loss observed at temperature ranges. In case of ZnAl LDH@AMCD the first mass loss of 0.33 % was observed between 30⁰C and 127⁰C. This nominal loss of mass is the primary characteristics of evaporation of physically adsorbed water molecules and most likely some weakly bound interstitial water within the ZnAl LDH@AMCD layers. The mass loss of low percentage implies that the ZnAl LDH@AMCD has reduced affinity for the absorption of moisture which could of the hydrophilic nature of carbon dots which may form a protective layer over the adsorbent surface. Second mass loss of 0.5 % occurred between 127⁰C and 191⁰C and this phase resembles the dehydroxylation of ZnAl LDH@AMCD structure wherein the hydroxyl groups are eliminated as water vapour. This minor mass loss indicates that the structural integrity of the adsorbent is relatively stable even in the presence of carbon dots. The interaction of the carbon dots with the metal cations might be playing a role in enhancing the stability of the hydroxide layers and thereby reducing its mobility. The third mass loss was observed between 191⁰C and 315⁰C with 1.3 % and this phase is accompanied with the decomposition of organic components encompassing the green carbon dots with the potential collapse of LDH structure. Along this temperature range this comparably low mass loss can be explained by the high thermal stability of the carbon dots. The carbon dots may contribute to the overall stability of the composite, limiting the extent of thermal degradation at high temperatures.

Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) was conducted on ZnAl LDH@AMCD, ZnAl LDH, CaAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH ( Fig. 6
) in order to estimate the material’s thermal stability and decomposition. This analysis aims to comprehend the effects of carbon dot intercalation on the LDH structure with a particular focus on the minimal loss observed at temperature ranges. As depicted from the [ Fig. 6 (b)] for pristine ZnAl LDH the first weight loss at 1.45 % occurs which is mainly associated with loss of water which is physically adsorbed and also water moisture. The second weight loss occurs at 0.57 %. This type of moderate water loss is potentially due to the dehydration of water molecules in the interlayers which are confined between the LDH layers. These shedding of water molecules occurs at slightly higher temperatures. The final weight loss for ZnAl LDH is associated with the dehydroxylation of the LDH structure alongwith the removal of coordinated water molecules. This weight loss is usually observed at elevated temperatures and pertains to the partial decomposition of the hydroxide layers. However upon mixing with green carbon dot solution, initial weight loss in ZnAl LDH@AMCD [ Fig. 6 (a)] due to surface moisture is substantially minimized to 0.35 %. This reduction is accredited to the hydrophobic nature of carbon dots, which may degrade the margins of water adsorption on the surface. The second weight loss occurs at 0.58 % which is comparably same in both the cases, thereby indicating that the incorporation of the carbon dots does not considerably affect the dehydration of water molecules. While the third weight loss exhibits a designated increase in case of ZnAl LDH@AMCD, suggesting that the incorporation of the carbon dot solution not only affects the thermal stability but also acquaints some additional thermal processes. This includes combustion or oxidation of the carbon dots at higher temperatures, which enhances the overall weight loss.

In case of CaAl LDH [ Fig. 6 (c)] the first weight loss at lower temperatures, 1.24 % occurs mainly due to the loss of physically adsorbed water. The minimal temperature range signifies the evaporation of water which are loosely bound to the surface. The second weight loss at 1.45 % is interrelated with the dehydration of water molecules in the interlayer spacing. The final weight loss at 1.26 % is because of the dehydroxylation of the LDH structure and also the prejudiced decomposition of the CaAl LDH framework. This step is mainly concerned with the removal of hydroxyl groups within the layers and thereby beginning th decomposition of the material at higher temperatures. Furthermore upon modification with the green carbon dots the first weight loss in CaAl LDH@AMCD 1.34 %, [ Fig. 6 (d)] is slightly higher as compared to that of pristine CaAl LDH which can be further attributed to the presence of carbon dots. This can mainly cause an increment in the surface area mainly available for moisture adsorption. Both the materials display similar second weight loss, although CaAl LDH@AMCD confines lower loss of 1.31 % as compared to the pristine CaAl LDH. The presence of carbon dots may intervene with the dehydration process, therby stabilizing the interlayer water. The final water loss is significantly lower in case of CaAl LDH@AMCD, 0.56 %. Carbon dots being thermally more stable may also buffer the LDH layers, thereby resulting in a reduced weight loss at higher temperatures.

Time-resolved Photoluminescence (TRPL) analysis was conducted in order to investigate the charge carrier lifetime and the recombination dynamics of both pristine LDH and carbon dots modified LDH. This technique helps allocate insights into the recombination times of excited carrier states. As illustrated in Fig. 7 . The average photoluminescence lifetime was found out to be 1.58 ns. This relatively elongated lifetime implies a slower recombination of charge carriers, implying that the pristine LDH has more extended excitation state. This signifies that such behaviour of a material reflects that the material has fewer defects or trap states which is beneficial for applications where there is advantage of prolonged charge separation. This longer lifetime permits the charge carriers to participate in chemical reactions or transfer processes before recombination occurs.

However in case of carbon dots mixed LDH, this exhibited a shorter lifetime of 0.7 ns. This reduction in lifetime of photoluminescence indicates a faster recombination of charge carriers. This decrease in lifetime is associated with the introduction of additional recombination centres or trap states due to the modification process. This frequently leads to the generation of surface states which assigns pathways for rapid non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes which is also one of the reasons in reduction of PL lifetime. This supports that the carbon dots are not only merely being adsorbed on the surface but are also interacting with the LDH structure, with the introduction of new dynamics in the charge carrier recombination process.

Photoluminescence analysis of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD is done as illustrated in Figure. All the adsorbents were analysed at different excitation wavelength to see how their emitted rays behave upon analysis. From the Fig. 8
(a), (b) and (c) it can be clearly seen that the carbon dot modified ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH are excited at different excitation wavelength and their emission were particularly found at 402 nm, 400 nm and 407 nm respectively. This slight difference in their emission wavelength suggests that the emissive centre i.e., carbon dots preserve their stable emission characteristics. Normally carbon dots show stable photoluminescence with minor shifts in emission wavelength which occurs primarily due to the surface states. The emission wavelengths corresponds to blue-violet light, a common characteristics of carbon dots. This minimal shift in emission spectra further elucidates that efficient energy is transferred from the pristine LDH matrix to carbon dots where the carbon dots act as emissive centres which enhances their surface states.

Whereas in case of ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH it has been found out that there has been emission at 377 nm, 380 nm and 386 nm as depicted in Fig. 8 (d), (e) and (f). This type of PL emission wavelength corresponds to the violet emission, being associated with band edge transitions in the pristine LDHs. Unlike the modified LDH they showed much less stable PL behaviour which is likely due to the lack of highly emissive centres.

The addition of certain amount of adsorbent dosage into the heavy metal solution can significantly impact the adsorption process. To assess the significance of adsorbent dosage on the removal of iron from aqueous solution, the experimental condition was fixed at initial metal concentration (Co) = 60 mg/L, volume = 50 mL, contact time = 240 min. The quantity of the adsorbent dosages was varied from 0.01gm to 0.4 gm for the ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD sorbent. From the study of the dosage parameters during the adsorption process for the removal of iron from the aqueous solution it has been found out that the adsorption capacity typically increased with the substantial increase in ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD dosage. Higher dose of the adsorbent provides more active sites for the iron ions to bind, enhancing the adsorption capacity. However, low adsorbent dosage leads to lower removal efficiency which occurs as a result of the availability of lesser active sites. Also with the increment of the adsorbent dosage there is more availability of the active sites allowing for greater interaction and adsorption. However it is also noticed that there is no significant change in the heavy metal removal efficiency beyond 0.2 g dosage. Nevertheless it can be confirmed that the highest removal efficiency was obtained at 0.2 gm of dosage. This may occur due to the agglomeration of the adsorbent particles at high adsorbent dose. This in turn reduces the availability of the external surface area and also to the strong limitations of mobility of Fe species in the adsorption medium thereby leaving some binding sites unsaturated. In addition the maximum adsorption capacity for ZnAl LDH@AMCD (87.09 %), Fig. 9
(c) and CaAl LDH@AMCD (77.72 %) Fig. 10
(c) was achieved at 0.2 g dosage. However in the case of pristine ZnAl LDH Fig. 9 (c) and CaAl LDH Fig. 10 (c) the removal percentage is quite low as compared to their modified form. One of the primary reasons for it can be the limited number of active adsorption sites. These materials lack sufficient functional groups which can effectively bond to their iron ions and consequently the adsorption process becomes less adequate leading to their lower overall removal percentage.

The equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) embodies the amount of the iron ions adsorbed per unit mass of the adsorbent. As it is clearly distinguished from Fig. 9 (b) the qe value is highest at low adsorbent dosage of 0.01 gm for ZnAl LDH (160.69 mg/g), ZnAl LDH@AMCD (176.03 mg/g). This is related to the limited number of adsorbent particles corresponding to the concentration of iron ions, which embarks on the paramount usage of the available active sites. Although the number of active sites increases with the gradual increase in adsorbent dosage but the concentration of iron ions in the solution remains constant. This phenomenon can be explained on the basis that upon increasing the dosage a saturation point is reached where the inclusion of more adsorbent does not result in significance increase in adsorption, while the excess adsorbent remains unutilized. However in case of CaAl LDH@AMCD (153.96 mg/g) and CaAl LDH (135.94 mg/g) it can be seen from Fig. 10 (b) that qe value is lower for CaAl LDH at the initial stages in comparison to CaAl LDH@AMCD but it increases with due increment of adsorbent dosage against pristine LDH. At initial lower dosage of CaAl LDH@AMCD, presence of increased functional groups leads to complexation and passivation of available surface sites. As a result the available surface area becomes saturated rapidly with iron ions which in turn reduces the effective adsorption capacity. In contrast the pristine CaAl LDH due to lesser availability of functional groups and more uniform surface area experiences a slower saturation process thereby leading to higher qe values. But at higher dosages of CaAl LDH@AMCD, the dispersion of the adsorbent progresses due to the larger number of available particles in the solution. As the liability of the aggregation of the adsorbent decreases, there occurs enhancement in the surface area with the presence of functional groups which help interact with the iron ions more effectively at higher dosages. With more availability of the adsorbent particles, saturation effect observed at lower dosages is mitigated resulting in more accessibility of the iron ions to larger number of adsorption sites thus preventing rapid surface saturation.

The effect of initial concentration onto the four adsorbents ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH, CaAl LDH@AMCD is plotted and presented in Fig. 9 (a), 10(a). The experiment was performed under the given conditions: (Co = 20––160 mg/L, dosages = 0.025 g, time = 240 min, volume = 50 mL). It has been observed that during the adsorption process for the removal of Iron from aqueous solution, it is noted that the adsorption capacity typically rises with the increase in the initial concentration of metal solution. This takes place as a result of the formation of greater driving force for mass transfer due to the higher initial concentration, which overcomes the resistance to adsorption onto the LDH surface. The high adsorption capacity of both ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD can be credited with several factors including the enhancement of the surface area and pore structure due to the integration of the carbon dot solution with the pristine LDH materials which ultimately provides more accessible adsorption sites. Carbon dots retaining larger surface area and being highly porous in nature offers additional binding sites which thereby enhances the adsorbents overall adsorption capacity. Furthermore the various functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl upon introduction of the carbon dot solution refines the adsorbents ability to form stronger interactions including hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking.

On the other hand, the pristine ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH has substantially lower surface area due to its confined surface area and fewer active sites. Due to the deficiency of the extra functional groups in both the pristine LDH adsorbent, the adsorbate molecules can only interact with the inherent surface sites of the LDH which are less reactive as well as fewer in number. Additionally the lack of surface functionalization in the pristine LDH reduces its ability to accommodate larger amounts if adsorbate molecules, thereby lowering its overall adsorption capacity.

The time dependent study for all the adsorbent is illustrated in Fig. 9 (d), 10(d). From the figure it can be seen that in case of ZnAl LDH and ZnAl LDH@AMCD, both the adsorbent display accelerated hike in the adsorption capacity during the first 15 min reaching qt value of 52.92 mg/g for the former and 55.19 mg/g for the later. This is associated with the abundance in the high-energy adsorption sites available at the beginning of the process. The qt value for both these adsorbents continue to increase at a slower rate indicating that the surface sites are progressively occupied and the iron ions must diffuse into the internal pores of the adsorbents. From 150 mins onwards qt value shows a minimal change for both the adsorbents indicating that all the accessible sites are occupied and the equilibrium is reached which slows down the adsorption rate significantly. Most of the high energy adsorption sites at this time are already engaged and the remaining sites are energetically less favourable leading to minimal changes. Furthermore ZnAl LDH@AMCD shows reasonably higher qt values as compared to its pristine form as because the introduction of carbon dots into the ZnAl LDH significantly increases the surface area leading to increased adsorption sites. Carbon dots which are rich in functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl and other oxygen containing moieties, can act as additional active sites for binding iron ions. This results in more efficient adsorption process especially in the later stages where diffusion into the internal layers of the adsorbent becomes important.

The adsorption behaviour of CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD for the time study was also studied for a time period of 240 min. In the initial 15 min the adsorption capacity(qt) for CaAl LDH@AMCD was found to be 64.35 mg/g and for CaAl LDH was found to be 56.56 mg/g. this implies a faster adsorption rate of the modified LDH as compared to the pristine CaAl LDH. The fusion of carbon dots with CaAl LDH causes an increment in surface area which in turn enhances and provides more active sites for the adsorption of iron ions, thereby allowing for a higher initial adsorption capacity. With the advancement of the adsorption process, both the adsorbents display a gradual increase in qt values. However during this entire intermediate phase, CaAl LDH exhibited a lower qt value suggesting that the introduction of the carbon dots enhances the entire adsorption process. Minimal change in qt values was monitored from 180 to 195 mins suggesting that the equilibrium was finally reached. However CaAl LDH@AMCD maintained higher qt values as compared to the pristine CaAl LDH due to the accumulation of the carbon dots onto the LDH surfaces. This contains unique surface functionalities such as carboxyl, hydroxyl which enhances their interaction with the metal ions. This improved affinity between the adsorbent and iron ions resulted in higher adsorption efficiency for CaAl LDH@AMCD.

Point of zero charge (PZC) is an important parameter in analyzing the surface chemistry and adsorptional behaviour of the adsorbent materials. It portrays the pH at which net surface charge of the material becomes zero which also impacts the interaction with ions in aqueous solutions. It also administers crucial information regarding the influence of the electrostatic properties of the adsorbent by the pH of the solutions. This study is important in order to understand how the adsorption process behaves with the change in pH. According to the investigation as illustrated in Fig. S1 ZnAl LDH@AMCD has pHPZC value of 7.93, ZnAl LDH has 6.84, CaAl LDH@AMCD has 7.82 and CaAl LDH has 7.68 repectively. This means that the adsorbents charge is positive when pH of the solution is below the pHPZC value and it is negative when pH value of the solution is above pHPZC value.

The pH value of the aqueous solution is a necessary factor that can influence the adsorption of iron ions. The adsorption of iron ions was investigated in the pH range of 2 to 12 alongwith the adsorbent dosages of 0.025 gm and concentration of 60 mg/L. The point of zero charge value (PZC) of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD was determined with the help of pH drift method and their respective values were determined at 6.84,7.93,7.68,7.82 as displayed in Fig. S1 . Normally it is believed that when pH > PZC, the charge at the surface of the asorbent becomes negative, whereas for pH < PHC, it acquires positive charge. As illustrated in Fig. 9 (f), 10(f) it can be clearly seen that at lower pH levels ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and ZnAl LDH shows lower removal percentage which occurs as a result of the competition between H+ ions and Fe3+ ions for active adsorption sites. Protonation of the adsorbent surface occurs as a result of the presence of excess H+ ions in acidic solutions. This results in electrostatic repulsion between the positively charged surface area and Fe3+ ions and consequently the removal percentage is low in acidic environment. However as the pH increases, the protonation of the surface decreases and the surface charge of the adsorbent shifts from positive to neutral and eventually negative. This consequences in better electrostatic attraction between negatively charged surface and Fe3+ ions. Nevertheless the modified LDH shows much better removal % than its pristine form in both acidic as well as alkaline environment due to the presence of various functional groups which increases the number of active adsorption sites. Especially at higher pH levels, where the surface is more negatively charged this functional groups can collaborate more effectively with the iron ions. Carbon dots also further increases the porosity of the adsorbent material thereby facilitating more diffusion of the iron ions into the adsorbent structure. This modification with the carbon dots further strengthen the ability of the adsorbent to attract and hold onto iron ions, even in the alkaline conditions, where the adsorption process is most favourable.

Thermodynamic studies are necessary for understanding the nature of adsorption, adsorption mechanism, spontaneity and surface heterogeneity of the adsorbent. In order to get a better understanding of the adsorption process due to the temperature effects, experiments were conducted at three different temperature ranges (313 K, 323 K and 333 K) with an initial heavy metal concentration of 60 mg/L and the adsorbent dosage was taken 0.025 gm as shown in Figure. The study indicated that the removal efficiency increases with the rise in temperature which can be attributed due to several factors. Higher the temperature higher will be the kinetic energy of the iron ions which promotes more frequent and effective collisions with the active sites on the surface of the ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD Fig. 9 (e), 10(e). Additionally the elevation of temperatures can increase the rate of diffusion of the iron ions within the aqueous solutions and into the pores of the adsorbent. It can also disrupt the weak bonding or resistance within the surface layer thereby allowing more iron ions to be effectively adsorbed. However it should also be noted that excessive high temperatures can also lead to the structural changes, promotion of endothermic adsorption processes or degradation of the LDH material which limits the improvement in the efficiency of the material. Thus understanding the effect of temperature on the adsorption capacity and rate allows for the optimization of the process conditions for maximum efficiency.

However in the case of the ZnAl LDH Fig. 9 (e) and CaAl LDH Fig. 10 (e) it is seen that the adsorption capacity is much lower in both the cases. This indicates that the adsorption process is more likely an exothermic one where lower temperature favors the process. With the increment in the temperature, the adsorbate molecules gaining mobility leads to the higher likelihood of desorption from the surface of the pristine LDH. Moreover the presence of fewer active sites and limited surface area of pristine adsorbent, compared to their modified counterparts reduces their ability to maintain adsorption efficiency at higher temperatures. Without the presence of the various functional groups, and surface modifications provided by carbon dots, the pristine adsorbents rely on weaker interactions such as Van der Waals forces which dimishes as the temperature rises.

The adsorption isotherm behaviour for removal of iron ions was estimated to differentiate the quantitative information regarding the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent and the distribution of iron ions between solid–liquid interfaces. The isothermal data was analyzed based on non-linear fitting Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin model. To evaluate the quality fit, both coefficient of determination (R2) and root mean square error (RMSE) were considered. The adsorption isotherm for the removal of iron ions using ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD fits well with the Langmuir model Fig. 9 (g), Fig. 10 (h). This indicates that the adsorption occurs on the homogeneous surface having a finite number of identical sites and thereby forming a monolayer of adsorbate molecules. The maximum adsorption capacity (Qmax) was found to be 287.38 mg/g for ZnAl LDH@AMCD and 186.35 mg/g for CaAl LDH@AMCD Table 3
(
Supplementary Information
). This demonstrates the efficiency of both these adsorbents is high in the uptake of iron ions from aqueous solutions. As shown in Table 6
(
Supplementary Information
), Langmuir model demonstrated best fir to the experimental data having high R2 value of 0.982 (ZnAl LDH@CD), 0.981 (CaAl LDH@CD) and lower RMSE value (1.34, 1.51) respectively. This suggests us that the adsorption sites on the prepared adsorbent are equivalent in energy and are uniformly distributed and this high correlation also further indicates the presence of strong affinity between the iron ions and both the adsorbents adsorption sites. Langmuir isotherm model further implies that the surface properties of the adsorbent are consistent, the adsorption process is highly predictable and that the adsorbent material displays significant potential as an efficacious adsorbent for heavy metal removal. This model also implies that, when once a site is occupied, no adsorption can occur further at that site thus indicating a monolayer coverage and is uniform in terms of its active sites. This type of characteristics makes ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD a promising candidate for water purification thereby further suggesting that it has a finite number of available vacant sites that are uniformly distributed across its surface area and that each one of it is capable of binding one iron ion.

However both the pristine ZnAl LDH, Fig. 9 (h) and CaAl LDH, Fig. 10 (h) follows Temkin adsorption isotherm model (R2 = 0.993), (R2 = 0.985) and lower RMSE values of (0.85, 1.38)
Table 6
(
Supplementary Information
), which indicates that the adsorption is characterized by uniform distribution of binding energies upto maximum binding energy. This model further demonstrates that adsorption decreases with increment in coverage, a phenomenon that arises from the interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent. These findings further highlights that the adsorption of iron ions onto ZnAl LDH is significantly influenced by these interactions reflecting a moderate binding affinity. Importantly as the surface approaches saturation, the energy of adsorption decreases in a gradual manner rather than in abrupt way.

The kinetic study for the removal of Iron (Fe) from aqueous solution using ZnAl LDH@AMCD contributes valuable insights into the adsorption mechanism and its process efficiency. Experimental data obtained from the kinetics study was interpreted with various non-linear models including Pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, Elovich and intraparticle diffusion model. Table 4
(
Supplementary information
) determines the values of the kinetic studies for the removal of iron (Fe) using the adsorbents. The non-linear fitting of the kinetics data based on these four models are shown in Fig. 9 (i,j) and Fig. 10 (i, j). Pseudo-First order kinetics model offers valuable insights into adsorption process of ZnAl LDH Fig. 9 (j) and CaAl LDH@CD Fig. 10 (i), which are primarily driven by physisorption. The fitting accuracy was assessed based on R2 and RMSE values. As shown in Table 7
(
Supplementary Information
) ZnAl LDH and CaAl LDH@CD demonstrated best fit to pseudo-first order model with high R2 value of 0.961 and 0,97 with lower RMSE values of 1.09 and 0.60 respectively. This model suggests us that the rate of adsorption relates directly to the number of unoccupied sites available on adsorbent surface. A notable aspect of this model is rapid initial adsorption phase which gradually slows down as more sites becomes occupied leading towards equilibrium,indicating relatively weak interactions between iron ions and adsorbent surface. This also further indicates us that availability of active sites serves as rate limiting factor, highlighting the adsorption process to be more surface controlled rather than reliant on the concentration of iron ions on the solution. It also indicates that for both these adsorbents the adsorption process is predominated by diffusion limited physical adsorption. Furthermore the model speculates that the number of vacant sites is directly proportional to the rate of occupation of adorption sites.

In contrast for ZnAl LDH@CD Fig. 9 (i) and CaAl LDH Fig. 10 (j) follows Pseudo-Second order kinetics model having high R2 value of 0.981 and 0.974 with low RMSE values of 0.66 and 0.61 respectively Table 7
(
Supplementary Information
). Thus suggesting a transition to different adsorption mechanism, which is primarily by chemisorptions. According to this model the rate of adsorption is proportional to the number of unoccupied sites, indicating a strong dependency on interactions between adsorbent and adsorbate. Initially the adsorption rate tends to be slower but it develops into more sustained process over time reflecting enhanced interactions between iron ions and adsorbent surface. This also suggests that electron sharing or exchange plays significant role in adsorption process leading to strong chemical interactions. Notably the modification of pristine ZnAl LDH into ZnAl LDH@CD has the potential to enhance surface properties, thus increasing the availability of functional groups that support chemisorptions, while CaAl LDH demonstrates similar characteristics even in its unmodified state. This type of chemisorptions processes are more distinct and slower thereby adhering well with the assumptions of pseudo second order kinetics.

The intraparticle diffusion model [ Fig. 9 (i,j), 10(i,j)] is widely used to determine whether the adsorption process is controlled primarily by diffusion of adsorbate molecules within pores of adsorbent or across its surface. In this model R2 value indicates how well the data aligns with diffusion process. For ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@CD, CaAl LDH, CaAl LDH@CD the R2 values were found to be 0.744, 0.73, 0.711,0.66 and low RMSE values 2.88, 2.49, 1.86, 2.02 respectively [
Table 7
(
Supplementary information
)]. The result suggests us that while intraparticle diffusion process plays a role in adsorption process, it is not the only rate influencing factor. Notably ZnAl LDH demonstrates highest R2 value indicating that pore diffusion is particularly significant here. The slightly lower R2 value for ZnAl LDH@CD indicates that incorporation of carbon dots may enhance the surface adsorption thereby reducing the reliance on this model, which is attributed to introduction of new functional groups. In contrast lower R2 value for CaAl LDH, CaAl LDH@CD as compared to Zn counterparts indicates a reduced impact of intraparticle diffusion in these systems.

Elovich model[ Fig. 9 (i,j), 10(i,j)] which effectively describes chemisorptions on heterogeneous surface, particularly with decrease with decrease in adsorption rate over time was also applied here in this study. The R2 values were notably higher, with values of 0.88 for ZnAl LDH, 0.857 for ZnAl LDH@CD, 0.864 for CaAl LDH and 0.826 for CaAl LDH@CD alongwith lower RMSE values 2.10, 1.63, 1.28, 1.45 respectively [
Table 4,7
(
Supplementary information
)]. These results support the conclusion that the Elovich model provides a better fit for the experimental data, emphasizing that chemisorptions significantly influences adsorption process. The α values reveals notable differences among the adsorbents: ZnAl LDH (62.39), ZnAL LDH@CD (63.55), CaAl LDH (101.14), CaAl LDH@CD (53.9) indicating a rapid initial adsorption rate, suggesting that Ca-based materials has higher intake for iron ions early in the process. Conversly, lower α values for CaAl LDH@CD points to potential reduction in initial adsorption rate due to steric hinderance or blockage of active sites caused by carbon dot modification. Interestingly, the Zn-based materials show more consistent α values, with modified ZnAl LDH@CD achieving a slightly increased rate (63.55) compared to the unmodified version (62.39). This could indicate that carbon dots enhance the initial adsorption efficiency in the ZnAl LDH system, possibly through an increase in surface area or improved surface interaction sites. Regarding the β values which indicates the rate of desorption, both ZnAl LDH and ZnAl LDH@CD displays value of 0.006 whereas for CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@CD are slightly higher at 0.007 and 0.009 respectively. This suggests us that desorption energy barrier is somewhat elevated for Ca-based adsorbents, particularly for carbon dot modification.

The reusability parameter is a significant for understanding the dependability and economic feasibility of the adsorbent. The reusability performance of the ZnAl LDH@AMCD, ZnAl LDH, CaAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH was performed upto seven cycles having the experimental condition of Co = 60 mg/L, dosages = 0.025 gm and contact time = 6 hr. After each successive cycle the initially adsorbed pristine and carbon dots modified LDH were regenerated using EDTA2 (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). The regenerated adsorbent was then further tested for 7 cycles which is depicted in Fig. 9 (k), 10(k). It is clearly evident from the figure that all the adsorbent showed a considerable decrease in heavy metal removal % after each cycle. Initially at first run the removal capacity of the adsorbent ZnAl LDH@AMCD (73 %), ZnAl LDH (67 %), CaAl LDH@AMCD (68 %) and CaAl LDH (61 %) further declined slowly to 47 %, 43 %, 44 % and 40 % respectively. EDTA is a hexadentate ligand which has a good and high capacity to bind heavy metals via complexation. Also by the end of 7th cycle the progressive decline in removal efficiency indicates the loss of adsorption sites with each reuse cycle. Nevertheless the green carbon dots modified ZnAl LDH@AMCD and CaAl LDH@AMCD displayed slightly better performances as compared to their pristine form suggesting that the modification with green carbon dots furnishes enhanced stability and resistance even though it diminishes with each cycle. This can also be associated with the increased number of active adsorption sites further improving surface hydrophilicity and potentially assigning additional functional groups capable of interaction with iron ions thereby maintaining LDH’s structural integrity.

In this work the materials from the d-block as well as s-block elements has been choosen in order to see which of the block elements has better adsorption capacity. Transition elements or otherwise known as d-block elements have unique electronic configurations, which are characterized by partially filled d-orbitals and are essential in both adsorption and formation of carbon dots. In carbon dot formation, transition metals administer active sites that promote the decomposition of organic precursors thereby promoting the formation of graphitic carbon structures within the carbon structures and hence significantly influencing the carbonization process. On the other hand s-block elements which mainly include alkali and alkaline earth metals, assist to adsorption and carbon dot synthesis through their ionic nature and basicity. However in the formation of carbon dot solution these s-block elements can play a supportive role which act as templates or stabilizing agents during the thermal carbonization of organic precursors. Additionally these elements when acquainted into the carbon dot structure imparts basic properties which remediate their dispersibility in aqueous solutions. Nevertheless various types of interactions takes place which makes the removal of iron ions more efficient. Upon modification with green carbon dots onto pristine LDH various functional groups present in carbon dots enhances the interaction with iron ions through hydrogen bonding which is crucial for capturing hydrated ions in solution. Also kinetics study aligned well with pseudo-first order model indicating physical adsorption mechanism at the surface. A significant factor in this process is the role of electrostatic interactions. Additionally for both the carbon dot modified adsorbents Van der Waals forces also support adhesion of iron ions. Presence of aromatic domains in carbon dots may also enable the π-π interactions. Also the possibility of ion–dipole interactions may also occur as a result of interactions between dipole moments of polar functional groups of carbon dot and iron ions. However incase of pristine LDH in the absence of carbon dots surface complexation takes place. Strong electrostatic interactions also takes place between negatively charged LDH surface and positively charged iron ions driving the adsorption onto the surface. Furthermore hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the LDH forms hydrogen bond with iron ions and water molecules thus stabilizing the adsorbed iron ions. Nevertheless Van der Waals interactions among the iron ions and surface of pristine LDH further furnishes additional support for iron adherence, ensuring that the iron ions remains adsorbed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD
ce:section.ce:para:
In the comparison of study among ZnAl LDH, ZnAl LDH@AMCD, CaAl LDH and CaAl LDH@AMCD for the removal of iron ions, it was found that the adsorption capacity of ZnAl LDH@AMCD was comparatively high as compared to the other adsorbents. From the Table 5
(
Supplementary Information
) it was found that CaAl LDH@AMCD exhibited highest qmax value. This is more likely due to the incorporation of carbon dots into its framework thereby enhancing the surface area, pore structure and active sites making it a highly effective in the adsorption of iron ions at high concentrations. It also enhances the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged iron ions and the negatively charged LDH layers. Nevertheless, Zinc(Zn) falling in the d block elements have a significant role in the enhancement of adsorption as compared to Calcium (s-block). The partially filled d-orbitals in case of Zn2+ permits for the formation of coordination bonds with iron ions thus leading to stronger binding interactions. Also it has high removal efficiency due to its enhanced surface chemistry, better accessibility of active sites at lower concentrations, which can be associated with favourable interactions with Fe3+ ions and functional groups from carbon dots. Furthermore d-block elements also takes part in complexation with the metal ions contributing to their higher stability.

Furthermore the impact of carbon dot loading amount was also studied and it was found that upon inclusion of higher amount of carbon dot solution onto ZnAl LDH it showed better removal percentage for heavy metal as it enhances its properties. The high carbon content bestows upon the development of more functionalized and interactive sites achieving removal efficiency upto 93.06 % with the increase in dosages as represented in Fig. S2 . Despite almost analogous carbon loading removal efficiency of iron ions for ZnAl LDH@AMCD is higher as compared to CaAl LDH@AMCD as because incase of former the bonding within the LDH layers allows to undergo better surface ion exchange thereby promoting higher interaction with Fe ions in aqueous solutions. Whereas in case of the later Ca2+ due to its larger ionic radius as well as higher charge density degrades the availability of active sites limiting its ion exchange and resulting in lower adsorption efficiency for Fe ions.

Although the removal iron ions were tested with various parameters with the prepared adsorbents, the river water taken contains various impurities alongwith the presence of various ionic species. River water contains various impurities in the form of dissolved salts (ions), organic substances, suspended particles (turbidity) and the presence of other cations (calcium, magnesium, sodium and potassium) and anions (chloride, sulphate and bicarbonate) which can compete for the active sites in adsorption. Besides turbidity can also block or clog the pores of the adsorbent material. Given these conditions the performance of all the adsorbents were evaluated for the removal of iron ions. The results depicted in Fig. S3 indicated that the prepared adsorbents were effective in targeting the iron ions directly from the river water. The LDH materials substantiated a remarkable ability to selectively adsorb the iron ions. Conjugating with their anion-exchange properties, this allowed them to exhibit selective iron removal. However their modified form because of the presence of functional groups, provides additional active sites and some degree of resistance against the organic substances and various impurities present in river water. This resistance occurs likely due to the hydrophilic nature of the functional groups on carbon dots which prevents the impurities from adhering to the surface, thereby keeping the adsorption sites available for iron ions.

Furthermore in order to assess the structural changes in the material after the adsorption of iron ions, the analysis of both the pristine and modified adsorbent was conducted via PXRD analysis. From Fig. S4
(a), incase of ZnAl LDH 2θ values were observed at 11.58⁰, 23.34⁰, 34.48⁰, 39.29⁰, 46.63⁰, 52.94⁰, 60.04⁰, 61.56⁰ corresponding to the planes at (003), (006), (104), (009), (113), (116), (208), (110) respectively. In Fig. S4 (b) for ZnAl LDH@AMCD have their 2θ values at 11.69⁰, 23.19⁰, 34.42⁰, 39.02⁰, 46.68⁰, 52.68⁰, 56.25⁰, 60.21⁰, 61.61⁰ corresponding to the planes at (003), (006), (104), (009), (202), (116), (211), (018), (214) respectively. The diffraction pattern revealed distinct peaks indicating the retention of the LDH even after the adsorption process. Notably the peak at (009) plane which was previously observed at 2θ values of 34⁰ shifted to 39.02⁰ for ZnAl LDH@AMCD and 39.29⁰ for ZnAl LDH signifying a change likely due to the interaction between the adsorbed iron ions and the LDH layers. Additionally two new peaks at 52.68⁰ (116) and 56.25⁰ (211) for carbon dot modified LDH and one single peak at 52.94⁰ (116) for pristine LDH were observed indicating structural reorganization induced by adsorption process. Despite these changes the peaks at (003), (006), (104) remained unchanged confirming the stability of the adsorbent.

However in case of CaAl LDH@AMCD Fig. S4 (c) the 2θ values were observed at 11.58⁰, 23.21⁰, 34.72⁰, 39.43⁰, 46.83⁰, 53.34⁰, 60.93⁰, 62.22⁰ having their planes corresponding at (003), (006), (200), (009), (220), (116), (018), (214) respectively. For CaAl LDH the 2θ values were observed at 11.86⁰, 23.30⁰, 34.92⁰, 39.33⁰, 46.74⁰, 60.94⁰, 62.01⁰ having their planes corresponding at (003), (006), (200), (009), (202), (018), (214) respectively. The diffraction patterns as illustrated in Fig. S4 (d) shows key reflections in the preservation of the LDH structure while revealing some notable shifts. The peaks at (003), (006), (200) remains stable reflecting the continued presence of the layered structure even after the adsorption. However the peak at 009 reflected a shift from its original 2θ value of 34⁰ to 39.33⁰ for CaAl LDH and 34⁰ to 39.43⁰ for CaAl LDH@AMCD indicating a change in interlayer spacing, suggesting the contraction of interlayer distance due to the interaction between the adsorbed iron ions and the LDH. Lastly the occurrence of new peaks at (202) for CaAl LDH and (116) for CaAl LDH@AMCD signifies the structural reorganization induced by adsorption process.

It is necessary to highlight that LDH is a layered material and that the dissolution of aluminium could disrupt the structure. In order to address this concern PXRD analysis was done after the removal of iron ions in alkaline conditions. The results displayed no significant changes in the crystallinity of the material assuring the layered structure of LDH remained intact through the adsorption process even after its modification. This further clearly indicates about the adsorbents resilience even in stronger alkaline conditions. Consequently while the dissolution of aluminium in strong alkaline conditions our findings robustly affirm that the adsorbent material maintains and retains its stability. This strongly reinforces the effectiveness of the LDH material in such conditions making it a reliable solution for heavy metal removal.

The cost analysis of the adsorbent is a crucial factor, emphasizing its economic and practical significance in adsorption studies, particularly for heavy metal removal from aqueous solution. The estimated synthesis cost of the prepared adsorbent was approximately 2 USD, providing a cost-effective alternative to expensive adsorbents, thereby enhancing the feasibility of large-scale applications. The Aegle Marmelos leaves were collected from local places and the grinding and drying process was done in the institution itself. Moreover, its high stability and ease of regeneration significantly reduce long-term costs by enabling multiple adsorption–desorption cycles. Notably, the majority of expenses were incurred during the characterization process of the material. Thus, the economic viability of this adsorbent underscores its potential for commercial-scale production, making it accessible for both research institutions and industrial sectors focused on environmental sustainability.

The findings from this study highlights the results that Zinc (Zn), a d-block element, demonstrates a significantly improved adsorption capacity as compared to Calcium (Ca) an s-block element. This enhanced performance is largely associated with the higher number of coordination sites available to Zinc and its ability to form more potential interactions with the metal ions, facilitating more efficient removal of iron ions. Nevertheless the introduction of carbon dots induces additional functional groups as well active sites for the removal of iron ions which further enhances the adsorption capacity. Furthermore future research should encourage more on enhancing the adsorption properties of these materials. Potential approach could involve doping of the Zinc based LDHs with other transition metals to further improve the adsorption efficiency. Moreover modifications in the interlayer structure through the incorporation of various organic and inorganic anions can be investigated to increase accessibility for metal ions. Such advancements could optimize zinc-aluminum LDH composites for broader environmental remediation applications, particularly in the context of removing heavy metals from contaminated water sources, ultimately contributing to more effective strategies for addressing water pollution challenges.
Manash Pratim Barman: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Hemaprobha Saikia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:simple-para.ce:italic: earned his Bachelor’s degree in Chemistry from Science College Kokrajhar, and subsequently completed his Master’s degree in Chemistry from Bodoland University. Currently, he is pursuing Ph.D at Bodoland University, where his research focuses on adsorption field. His primary areas of interest include the removal of dyes and heavy metals from aqueous solutions and the utilization of Layered Double Hydroxide (LDH) as an adsorbent material. Additionally, he has also explored some of the work with Bentonite. Through his work, Manash Pratim Barman aims to contribute to the development of efficient and sustainable solutions for environmental remediation challenges. (Orcid Id- 0009-0009-7195-0496)

, a resident of Kokrajhar, Assam, India, completed her B.Sc. in Chemistry from Handique Girls’ College, Gauhati University, in 2003. She earned her M.Sc. in Inorganic Chemistry from Assam University, Silchar, in 2006, followed by a B.Ed. from Gossaigaon B.Ed. College in 2009. In November 2013, she obtained her Ph.D. from Gauhati University, Guwahati. Since 2015, Dr. Saikia has been serving as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemistry at Bodoland University, Kokrajhar. Her research interest includes Bentonite, Catalysis, Electrocatalysis, Adsorption, Antibacterial, Nanoparticles and Layered Double Hydroxides (LDH), an area in which she has significant expertise. (Orcid Id- 0000-0002-8714-8766)
